title: Hook that detects 'foldable in tabletop posture' for split top/bottom UI
viewport_spectrum: Galaxy Z Fold/Flip in half-open (laptop) posture, Surface Duo similar
scenario_context: Foldable in laptop/tabletop posture has screen split horizontally by hinge — top half is for content display, bottom half for controls (like a physical laptop). Detect this posture and lay out accordingly.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Treating laptop posture as full unfolded
responsive_reasoning: 1. Vertical viewport segments indicate horizontal fold (laptop posture).\n2. Use top segment for video/content, bottom for controls.\n3. Fall back gracefully on non-foldable browsers.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS grid rows aligned to viewport segments

performance_notes: Pure CSS. No JS.
accessibility_across_viewports: main + aside landmarks preserved. Tab order top to bottom.
edge_cases_handled: Fold device flipped flat — no fold detected, single column
Tabletop posture — clear top/bottom split
Non-foldable browser — fallback single column
Foldable in book posture (vertical fold) — different media query applies
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single column across fold has controls hidden by hinge crease or content awkwardly split.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Hinge-aware split. 2) Native CSS environment variables. 3) Graceful fallback.

title: Sidebar widget that adapts content based on its width while sidebar itself is user-resizable
viewport_spectrum: Widget inside resizable sidebar (180px to 480px range)
scenario_context: Resizable sidebar with widgets. User drags sidebar wider — widgets should adapt: at 180px just icons, at 280px icon+label, at 380px+ full content. The viewport is irrelevant.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: JS-based width measurement (CSS native is better)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Sidebar is a container; widgets adapt to its width.\n2. Container queries are perfect for this.\n3. User-resized width drives layout.

layout_strategy.content_priority: Always: icon. Wider: label. Widest: stats

interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Resize sidebar to control widget detail

performance_notes: Pure CSS. Resizing sidebar triggers single layout pass.
accessibility_across_viewports: Always-present icon ensures hover/title gives full info even when label hidden. Touch target h-11.
edge_cases_handled: User drags sidebar to 180px — only icon visible, title via tooltip
User drags to 400px — full widget with stats
Multiple widgets — all adapt simultaneously
what_breaks_naive_approach: JS-driven sidebar width measurement re-renders all widgets on every drag pixel. Container queries adapt natively.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Widget adapts to sidebar width. 2) Multiple density levels. 3) Pure CSS.

title: Numeric values display with tabular-nums and fluid sizing for dashboards
viewport_spectrum: Mobile to 4K — numeric data must align across all
scenario_context: Dashboard metrics like '$1,234,567.89' should align vertically and scale smoothly. tabular-nums ensures consistent digit width; clamp() handles fluid size.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Proportional digits (numbers don't align across rows)
Forgetting to enable font feature on display fonts
responsive_reasoning: 1. Numeric alignment requires tabular-nums (monospace digits).\n2. Display size scales fluidly via clamp.\n3. font-variant-numeric is the canonical CSS property.
performance_notes: Pure CSS. Intl.NumberFormat is native and fast. No JS layout.
accessibility_across_viewports: Currency formatted by locale. Numbers readable at any size via fluid clamp.
edge_cases_handled: Very large numbers — tabular-nums keeps alignment
Different currencies — Intl handles
Negative percentages — color + sign change
Mobile viewport — number scales smaller but stays legible
what_breaks_naive_approach: Proportional digits make numbers misalign across rows in a list. Fixed font size doesn't scale.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) tabular-nums for column alignment. 2) Fluid sizing. 3) Locale-aware formatting.

title: Card grid using grid-template-columns auto-fit minmax for content-driven column counts
viewport_spectrum: Any viewport — grid adapts based on container and minimum card width
scenario_context: A grid of cards. Naive approach hardcodes column counts per breakpoint (1 col mobile, 2 col tablet, 3 col desktop). Better: declare minimum card width — browser fills as many columns as fit.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Fixed card width that doesn't fill row
responsive_reasoning: 1. auto-fit + minmax = browser decides columns based on minimum card width.\n2. No breakpoints needed.\n3. Works at any viewport from 320px to 4K.

layout_strategy.information_density_changes: More columns at wider viewports automatically

interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab through cards in DOM order

performance_notes: Pure CSS. Single grid layout pass.
accessibility_across_viewports: DOM order respected for Tab/screen reader.
edge_cases_handled: Single card — still 1fr (full width)
Many cards on 4K — many columns
Container narrower than min — fallback via min(280px, 100%)
Gap eats column — min() ensures no overflow
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hardcoded column counts at fixed breakpoints leave awkward gaps at intermediate widths. auto-fit fills space naturally.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Zero breakpoints. 2) Min card width drives column count. 3) Single-line CSS solution.

title: Resize cursor hint that only appears for precise pointers
viewport_spectrum: Any device with input variation
scenario_context: A resizable panel divider. On mouse, show 'col-resize' cursor on hover so user knows to drag. On touch, the cursor doesn't exist — touch users need different affordance (visible grab handle).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hover affordance only (touch users can't discover)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Cursor change is mouse-specific affordance.\n2. Touch needs persistent visual handle.\n3. (pointer: fine) shows cursor; (pointer: coarse) shows handle dots.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Drag handle dots visible always; drag to resize
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same; with trackpad gets cursor too
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Cursor changes on hover; resize handles smaller

performance_notes: Pure CSS for affordance switching. No JS overhead.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=separator + tabIndex enables keyboard resize. Handle dots aria-hidden (decorative).
edge_cases_handled: Hybrid device with both inputs — both affordances active
Touch user with stylus — pointer:fine likely, gets cursor (correct)
Mouse user on touchscreen laptop — both available, both shown
what_breaks_naive_approach: cursor: col-resize is invisible to touch users — no affordance. Always-visible handles look noisy on desktop.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Cursor for mouse, dots for touch. 2) Hybrid devices get both. 3) Same component.

title: Bottom sheet that grows to fit keyboard-summoned content without breaking
viewport_spectrum: Mobile bottom sheets with form inside
scenario_context: A bottom sheet has a form. When user focuses input, keyboard appears, sheet's bottom is hidden. Need sheet to either: (a) translate up with keyboard, or (b) be fullscreen when keyboard active.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Sheet that stays bottom-anchored under keyboard
Fullscreen sheet always (loses native sheet UX)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Sheet height + keyboard inset > viewport height → must convert to fullscreen.\n2. visualViewport gives keyboard inset.\n3. Sheet snaps to fullscreen when keyboard appears.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Sheet transitions to fullscreen when keyboard summoned
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Sheet grows when keyboard takes screen space

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Sheet opens half-screen → tap input → expands fullscreen with keyboard

performance_notes: Transitions GPU-accelerated. Single visualViewport listener.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=dialog. Focus traps if implemented. Keyboard summoned in form input as expected.
edge_cases_handled: Form inside sheet — fullscreen when input focused
Multiple inputs — sheet stays fullscreen across focus changes
User dismisses keyboard — sheet collapses back to bottom sheet
Very tall content — sheet scrolls internally
what_breaks_naive_approach: Bottom sheet with max-h: 85dvh has half its content hidden behind keyboard. Manual scroll fixes feel janky.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Sheet adapts to keyboard state automatically. 2) Fullscreen mode preserves access to all controls. 3) Returns to native sheet UX when keyboard dismissed.

title: Custom video player controls that respect home indicator and notch in fullscreen
viewport_spectrum: iPhone fullscreen video playback with notch + home indicator
scenario_context: Video player in fullscreen on iPhone. Controls overlay should not be obscured by home indicator (bottom) or notch (top/sides in landscape).
responsive_reasoning: 1. Fullscreen video extends edge-to-edge; controls must respect safe areas.\n2. Bottom controls (scrubber) must clear home indicator.\n3. Top controls (close, settings) must clear notch.

layout_strategy.content_priority: Video full-bleed; controls in safe zone

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap video to toggle controls, controls in safe zone
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover shows controls; safe areas usually 0

performance_notes: Pure CSS, GPU opacity transitions.
accessibility_across_viewports: Controls accessible in any orientation. Touch targets 44pt.
edge_cases_handled: Landscape with notch on left — left padding shifts controls right
iPad with home indicator — bottom padding clears it
Browser without env support — max() floor padding
what_breaks_naive_approach: Controls flush to bottom edge are partially under home indicator — scrubber unreachable.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) All four safe areas respected. 2) Works in any orientation. 3) max() ensures padding even without env.

title: Game UI that flips between portrait HUD and landscape HUD without losing state
viewport_spectrum: Phone portrait, phone landscape (forced for games)
scenario_context: A web-based casual game. In portrait, HUD elements (score, time, controls) wrap top-and-bottom. In landscape, they go on left/right edges. Same game state, different UI placement.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Game canvas is the priority; HUD wraps around.\n2. Portrait: top+bottom HUD. Landscape: left+right HUD.\n3. Same React state, just CSS reflow.

interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: WASD or arrow keys for movement

solution:
// components/GameLayout.tsx export function GameLayout({ canvas, scoreHud, controlsHud }: { canvas: React.ReactNode; scoreHud: React.ReactNode; controlsHud: React.ReactNode }) { return (
{scoreHud}
{canvas}
{controlsHud}
); }
performance_notes: Pure CSS layout. Game state in React; visuals via canvas. No remount on rotation.
accessibility_across_viewports: Game canvas needs role=application + aria-label. Score live region for screen readers.
edge_cases_handled: Rotation mid-game — layout adapts, game continues
Landscape phone — HUD wraps tight to edges, canvas maximized
iPad in landscape — generous HUD space
User rotates back — state preserved
what_breaks_naive_approach: Same HUD position in both orientations either crowds portrait or wastes landscape space. Different components per orientation duplicates state.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) HUD reflows around canvas per orientation. 2) Safe area respected on each edge. 3) State preserved across rotation.

title: Tablet keyboard accessory bar (Previous/Next field, Done) that doesn't conflict with sticky elements
viewport_spectrum: iPad with Magic Keyboard accessory bar, iPhone with input accessory view
scenario_context: iPad displays an input accessory bar above its keyboard with Prev/Next/Done buttons. Custom web forms can't use this native bar but should mirror its behavior with web-native equivalents.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No way to skip required fields
Done button that doesn't dismiss keyboard
responsive_reasoning: 1. iPad users expect accessory bar interactions; web forms must mimic with own UI.\n2. Floating bar at top of keyboard (visualViewport-aware).\n3. Prev/Next traverse focusable inputs; Done blurs current input.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Floating accessory bar via visualViewport positioning

interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same plus native input accessory might already exist (web bar coexists)

performance_notes: Single visualViewport listener. Direct DOM query for inputs (fast).
accessibility_across_viewports: Buttons have aria-label. 44pt touch targets. Done blurs focus, dismissing keyboard.
edge_cases_handled: No more inputs after current — Next does nothing (could disable)
Keyboard dismissed — bar hides
User taps Done — keyboard closes
Form with hidden inputs — querySelector filters visible
what_breaks_naive_approach: No accessory bar means users on web forms can't easily skip between fields like they do in native apps. The keyboard's native accessory (where available) might not include web form fields.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Floating bar tied to keyboard inset. 2) Field navigation works across input types. 3) Done button dismisses keyboard reliably.

title: Pane that toggles between full-bleed and constrained based on content type and viewport
viewport_spectrum: Various — wide content needs full-bleed; reading content needs constrained
scenario_context: An admin app where the main pane shows different content types. Dashboards/tables want full-bleed. Documentation pages want constrained max-width: 75ch for readability. The pane itself decides based on content type.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Always max-width: 1200px (wastes ultrawide space for dashboards)
Always full-bleed (text too wide to read)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Content type drives width — data wants width, prose wants constraint.\n2. Two pane variants: 'wide' (full container) and 'reading' (max-width).\n3. Single pane component, content-type prop.
performance_notes: Pure CSS. Single layout pass.
accessibility_across_viewports: main landmark preserved. Reading width prevents eye strain on long lines.
edge_cases_handled: Mobile — full width either way
Ultrawide with reading content — centered with margins
Mixed content (table inside prose) — table can break out via class
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single fixed max-width either wastes ultrawide for dashboards or makes prose unreadable. Variant-driven is the solution.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Content-aware width. 2) Single component. 3) Fluid padding scales appropriately.

title: Empty state for a table that adapts message and CTA per viewport
viewport_spectrum: Mobile, tablet, desktop empty states
scenario_context: When a table has no data, show empty state. On mobile, compact illustration + single CTA. On tablet/desktop, larger illustration + supporting text + multiple actions.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Same empty state at every size (cramped on mobile)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Empty state should fit available space without crowding.\n2. Mobile gets essential CTA; desktop gets full context.\n3. Illustration scales fluidly.

layout_strategy.content_priority: Primary CTA always; secondary at wider

performance_notes: Pure CSS with container queries. Illustration loaded once.
accessibility_across_viewports: Decorative illustration with alt=''. Real button CTAs.
edge_cases_handled: Narrow container (sidebar) — compact state
Wide main pane — full state with both CTAs
No import option — secondary action absent
what_breaks_naive_approach: Same state cramps mobile. Hiding secondary action by viewport (not container) means narrow main pane on desktop also gets cramped.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Container-driven disclosure. 2) Progressive enhancement of content. 3) Adapts to its actual slot.

title: OTP input with 6 segments that auto-advances and pastes properly across devices
viewport_spectrum: Mobile, tablet, desktop OTP entry
scenario_context: 6-digit verification code input. Mobile users get SMS — iOS auto-fills 6 digits. Should auto-distribute pasted code across segments. Each digit input has numeric keyboard.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single input that doesn't visually segment
responsive_reasoning: 1. OTP UX is universal — 6 segments showing entry progress.\n2. Mobile autofill from SMS pastes whole code at once.\n3. autoComplete='one-time-code' triggers iOS suggestion bar.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: iOS auto-fills from SMS via suggestion above keyboard. Tap to type manually.
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab/Shift+Tab between, Backspace clears and goes back

performance_notes: Direct DOM focus management. Single state array. No expensive re-renders during paste.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=group with aria-label. Each input has aria-label. Numeric keyboard via inputMode + pattern. one-time-code on first input enables iOS autofill.
edge_cases_handled: iOS SMS auto-fill — paste distributes across 6 segments
Backspace at empty segment — goes to previous
Arrow keys — navigate without typing
User pastes 6 digits in 3rd segment — fills from there forward
User pastes 10 digits — fills first 6, drops rest
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single input without segmentation looks wrong for OTP entry. Segments that don't support paste require manual typing — slow.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) iOS SMS autofill works seamlessly via autoComplete='one-time-code'. 2) Paste distributes across segments. 3) Arrow keys + Backspace navigation matches user expectations.

title: Avatar component that uses small/medium/large image based on render size, not viewport
viewport_spectrum: Avatars rendered at varied sizes throughout the app
scenario_context: User avatars appear in: chat thread (24px), profile card (64px), profile page hero (128px). Should request appropriately-sized images at each rendering. srcset with sizes handles it.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single avatar URL at largest size (wasted bandwidth)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Avatar size known at component level — srcset + sizes communicates intent.\n2. Browser picks 2x for retina displays.\n3. Image CDN handles resize on request.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: srcset with explicit sizes per component variant

performance_notes: Smaller avatars load smaller files. Browser picks 1x/2x/3x based on DPR. lazy loading defers off-screen avatars.
accessibility_across_viewports: alt text is user name. width/height prevents CLS.
edge_cases_handled: 3x DPR iPhone — 144px image for 48px avatar (sharp)
1x desktop — 48px image (small file)
Many avatars in chat (24px) — minimal bandwidth
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single 128px URL means 24px chat avatars download 5x more data than needed.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Right-sized requests per render context. 2) DPR-aware. 3) Lazy loaded.

title: Pagination that becomes 'Load more' on mobile and full controls on desktop
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (Load more button), tablet (compact pagination), desktop (full)
scenario_context: List pagination. Desktop shows '<< Prev | 1 2 3 ... 10 | Next >>'. Mobile shows 'Load more' button (infinite-scroll feel). Same paginated data, different UI.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Tiny page number buttons on mobile (hard to tap)
Infinite scroll without manual control (accessibility issue)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Desktop benefits from random page access. Mobile prefers sequential loading.\n2. 'Load more' is a single accessible CTA replacing multiple buttons.\n3. Same data; different ergonomics.

layout_strategy.content_priority: Always: way to load more. Desktop: way to jump.
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Desktop shows page count; mobile shows progress

performance_notes: Single component handles both modes. Page truncation prevents 100 page buttons on long lists.
accessibility_across_viewports: nav with aria-label. aria-current=page. Each button aria-label. Disabled state at edges.
edge_cases_handled: First/last page — Prev/Next disabled
Many pages — '...' truncation
Mobile — single button keeps focus simple
User reaches end — Load more disappears
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tiny page number buttons on mobile fail tap targets. Pure infinite scroll without controls is accessibility issue.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Random access on desktop, sequential on mobile. 2) Single accessible CTA per mode. 3) Truncation prevents UI explosion.

title: Toast notification that stacks at top on mobile and bottom-right on desktop
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (top, full-width), desktop (bottom-right corner)
scenario_context: App toasts. On mobile, top stack is conventional (matches iOS notifications, doesn't conflict with bottom nav). On desktop, bottom-right is unobtrusive. Both stacks: newest pushes older down/up.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Bottom toasts on mobile (conflict with bottom nav, home indicator)
Toasts that block important UI permanently
responsive_reasoning: 1. Toast position respects what's at the edge — mobile bottom has nav/home indicator. Desktop bottom is free.\n2. Same toast data, different positioning.\n3. Stack direction matches position.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Adaptive position with same toast component
layout_strategy.content_priority: Newest at top of stack visually

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Swipe up to dismiss; auto-dismiss after 5s
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: X button or auto-dismiss; hover pauses timer

performance_notes: aria-live=polite for non-disruptive announcements. pointer-events handling allows clicks through gaps. Single container for all toasts.
accessibility_across_viewports: aria-live region. role=status per toast. Dismiss button 44pt-ish (h-8 w-8 with padding compensation in production).
edge_cases_handled: Many toasts — stack respects max-h via container
Mobile rotation — toasts reposition to new top safe area
User has reduced-motion — no slide animation
Toast with very long message — wraps within max width
what_breaks_naive_approach: Bottom toasts on iPhone hide behind home indicator. Same position everywhere conflicts with respective UI conventions.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Position respects viewport-specific UI conventions. 2) Safe area aware. 3) Same toast data, two positions.

title: Browser zoom 200% scenario: layout must remain functional and legible
viewport_spectrum: Any viewport at 200% zoom (effective half size)
scenario_context: WCAG requires layouts to work at 200% zoom without horizontal scrolling or loss of content. Many designs break: fixed-width elements overflow, sticky elements pile up. Need a layout that respects zoom.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Fixed widths in px (overflow at zoom)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Zoom is a viewport change. 200% zoom on 1280px screen = 640px effective viewport.\n2. Use rem/em/% throughout — they scale with zoom.\n3. Test at 320 CSS px width AT 200% zoom (= 640px equivalent).

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Relative units throughout + responsive max widths

performance_notes: rem-based design has no perf cost.
accessibility_across_viewports: WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.10 (Reflow) requires content reflows at 320 CSS px width without horizontal scroll. SC 1.4.12 (Text Spacing) requires text spacing adjustments don't break layout.
edge_cases_handled: 200% zoom — layout reflows like at narrow viewport
User with custom font size 24px — entire design scales up
User with text-spacing extension — gap/line-height tolerate increases
what_breaks_naive_approach: max-width: 1200px ignores zoom — at 200% zoom on 1280px screen, the 1200px container still tries to be 1200px CSS pixels, overflowing the 640px effective viewport. Fixed px sizes throughout cascade the problem.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) rem-based units honor zoom and user font size. 2) WCAG-compliant at 200% zoom. 3) Scales with user accessibility preferences.

title: Detect virtual keyboard state independently of visualViewport (Android quirks)
viewport_spectrum: Android with floating keyboards, iOS with various keyboard types
scenario_context: Android keyboards behave differently than iOS — some Android keyboards push content (resize layout viewport), some float (no resize). visualViewport handles iOS but not always Android. VirtualKeyboard API (Chrome) is more reliable on Android.
responsive_reasoning: 1. navigator.virtualKeyboard.overlaysContent = true opts in to overlay behavior.\n2. virtualKeyboard.boundingRect gives precise keyboard area.\n3. CSS env(keyboard-inset-height) when API available.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Progressive enhancement: VirtualKeyboard API > visualViewport > resize event

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Form fields scroll into view above keyboard

performance_notes: VirtualKeyboard API is native — no polling. visualViewport fallback adds rAF throttle. Single source of inset.
accessibility_across_viewports: Keyboard inset awareness allows focused input to remain visible — no users typing into a hidden input.
edge_cases_handled: Chrome with VirtualKeyboard API — uses overlay mode + env() var
Safari iOS — visualViewport fallback
Android floating keyboard — VirtualKeyboard API gives accurate rect
Browser without either — falls back to default (no inset)
what_breaks_naive_approach: visualViewport doesn't fire for all Android keyboards. VirtualKeyboard API solves this on Chrome. Combined approach handles both platforms.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) VirtualKeyboard API (modern Chrome). 2) visualViewport fallback (iOS). 3) Progressive enhancement chain.

title: Pricing cards layout that switches between row and column based on container
viewport_spectrum: Pricing rendered in homepage section (wide), embed (narrow), modal (medium)
scenario_context: 3 pricing tiers shown. In wide container, horizontal row (each tier side by side). In narrow container, vertical stack with featured one larger. Same content, different emphasis.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Viewport breakpoints (irrelevant — pricing might be in embed)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Container size drives whether tiers fit side-by-side.\n2. Container query determines row vs column.\n3. Featured tier emphasis adapts.
performance_notes: Pure CSS container queries.
accessibility_across_viewports: ul/li structure. Each tier focusable via CTA button. Featured tier visually distinct.
edge_cases_handled: Embed in narrow sidebar — stacks vertically
Homepage section wide — row layout
Modal medium — adapts based on modal width
4 tiers — flex-1 distributes
what_breaks_naive_approach: Viewport-based: embed in narrow sidebar at desktop viewport gets cramped row layout. Container queries solve this.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Container-aware layout. 2) Featured tier emphasis adapts. 3) Same in any embed context.

title: Heading that shrinks aggressively at narrow viewports to prevent overflow
viewport_spectrum: 320px Galaxy Fold closed to 4K
scenario_context: A page hero heading 'Welcome to our amazing platform that changes everything' might wrap awkwardly or overflow at narrow viewports. Need a fluid scale with aggressive narrow-viewport floor.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Linear clamp() that scales too gently (overflow at narrow)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Long heading text needs more aggressive minimum at narrow viewports.\n2. clamp() lower bound forces a usable minimum.\n3. text-wrap: balance distributes lines visually.

layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Narrow viewports show smaller heading; content adapts

performance_notes: Pure CSS. text-wrap: balance is browser-computed once.
accessibility_across_viewports: Real h1. Hyphens enable word-break in long words without breaking semantic.
edge_cases_handled: Very long heading at 320px — wraps with hyphens, no overflow
Short heading — balance ensures even line lengths
Heading with single very long word — word-break: break-word handles
Right-to-left languages — hyphens work in supported languages
what_breaks_naive_approach: Without aggressive minimum, headings overflow viewport on Galaxy Fold (280px). text-wrap: balance not used means awkward 1-word-on-last-line orphans.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Aggressive narrow-viewport floor. 2) text-wrap: balance for even lines. 3) Hyphenation prevents overflow.

title: Sidebar gap that respects container width via container queries (not viewport)
viewport_spectrum: Sidebars at varied widths in various contexts
scenario_context: A sidebar's internal gap between items should be smaller when sidebar is narrow (icons only) and larger when expanded (icons + labels). Container queries on sidebar drive gap.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Fixed gaps for all sidebar widths
responsive_reasoning: 1. Gap inside sidebar depends on sidebar width, not viewport.\n2. Container query on sidebar adjusts gap token.\n3. Tighter gap when narrow (icons close), looser when wide.
performance_notes: Pure CSS.
accessibility_across_viewports: Touch targets maintain 44pt regardless of gap.
edge_cases_handled: Sidebar 60px (rail) — gap 0.25rem
Sidebar 240px — gap 0.75rem
Resizing — gap adapts smoothly
what_breaks_naive_approach: Same gap at 60px (cramped) and 320px (sparse).
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Gap adapts to sidebar density. 2) Container-driven. 3) Maintains tap targets.

title: Swipe gesture for navigation that doesn't conflict with browser back-swipe
viewport_spectrum: iOS/Android with edge-swipe back gesture
scenario_context: App has swipe-left to delete a list item. iOS Safari/Chrome have edge-swipe-from-left to go back. Need to detect: is swipe starting near edge (let browser handle) or mid-item (handle ourselves).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Blocking all swipe at edges (breaks browser nav)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Edge zone (<20px from left/right) reserved for browser gestures.\n2. Item swipe starts only outside edge zone.\n3. touch-action: pan-y prevents browser hijacking horizontal swipes mid-list.

layout_strategy.content_priority: Browser nav primary; app gesture secondary

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Mid-item swipe reveals actions; edge swipe goes back

performance_notes: Pointer events, transform-based animation (GPU). touch-action: pan-y allows vertical scroll, captures horizontal.
accessibility_across_viewports: Touch gesture has keyboard alternative (Delete key when focused on item — not shown). Screen reader users have explicit delete button accessible via context menu.
edge_cases_handled: User starts swipe at edge — browser back gesture works
User swipes mid-item — reveal/delete works
Multiple items revealed — only one at a time (close others on new swipe)
Diagonal swipe — touch-action pan-y captures only horizontal
what_breaks_naive_approach: Blocking all swipes at left edge prevents browser back gesture. Allowing both creates ambiguous gesture conflict.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Edge zone reserved for browser. 2) Mid-item zone for app gestures. 3) touch-action: pan-y for proper gesture isolation.

title: Modal that closes when user dismisses keyboard via swipe-down on iOS
viewport_spectrum: iOS modals with input focus
scenario_context: iOS keyboard can be swiped down to dismiss. When this happens in a modal form, user usually wants the modal to remain open. But if user dismisses keyboard then taps backdrop, modal should close. Distinguish keyboard dismissal from modal dismissal.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Keyboard dismissal != modal dismissal.\n2. visualViewport resize event = keyboard state change; not user intent to close modal.\n3. Backdrop click = modal close.

detection_strategy.code_pattern: // Only close on explicit backdrop click or close button

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Modal state independent of keyboard state
layout_strategy.content_priority: Modal stays open during keyboard transitions
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Modal height may adapt when keyboard appears/disappears

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Swipe keyboard down keeps modal; tap backdrop closes modal
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Click backdrop or X to close

performance_notes: Single global keyboard inset listener. Modal close handlers explicit (no inferring from viewport changes).
accessibility_across_viewports: role=dialog aria-modal. Escape closes. Backdrop click closes. Content click contained.
edge_cases_handled: User dismisses keyboard via swipe — modal stays open, slides back to bottom
User taps backdrop — modal closes
User taps input — keyboard appears, modal slides up
User taps content area — no close (event stopped)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Closing modal on visualViewport.resize closes modal when keyboard dismisses — unexpected to user.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Modal state decoupled from keyboard state. 2) Explicit close intents only. 3) Smooth animations as keyboard transitions.

title: Loading skeleton that respects safe areas and viewport in full-screen splash
viewport_spectrum: iOS/Android with notch/home indicator/gesture pill during loading
scenario_context: App initial loading shows skeleton placeholders. Full-screen splash skeleton extends edge-to-edge with content placeholders inside safe areas. Should match the final layout's safe-area handling.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Skeleton edge-to-edge without safe area (looks broken)
Skeleton without matching final layout (jarring swap on load)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Skeleton should preview final layout, including safe area respect.\n2. Same env() values everywhere.\n3. Smooth transition from skeleton to content.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Skeleton matches final layout with same padding
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Skeleton placeholders match final element sizes

performance_notes: CSS animation-based pulse — GPU. No JS.
accessibility_across_viewports: aria-busy='true' + aria-live='polite' + aria-label tells screen readers content is loading.
edge_cases_handled: Notch — skeleton respects top inset
Home indicator — skeleton respects bottom inset
Reduced-motion — pulse animation skipped via prefers-reduced-motion
what_breaks_naive_approach: Skeleton without safe-area awareness shows placeholders under notch — visually breaks on iPhone.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Skeleton matches final layout's safe area handling. 2) Smooth content swap. 3) Accessible loading state.

title: Carousel that shows 1.5 cards on phone portrait and 3.5 cards on tablet landscape
viewport_spectrum: Phone portrait (peek of next), tablet landscape (multiple visible)
scenario_context: Horizontal scrolling carousel. Showing partial card on edge tells user there's more (1.5 cards). Number of visible cards depends on viewport AND orientation.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Partial card peeks indicate scroll affordance — universal UX pattern.\n2. Card width = viewport / (visible count) — fluid.\n3. CSS scroll-snap aligns scrolling.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: flex-basis with division by visible count
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: More visible per row at wider viewports

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Swipe horizontally, scroll-snap aligns to next card
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Arrow keys move focus through cards; scrolls into view

performance_notes: CSS-only carousel — no JS. Scroll-snap provides native momentum and alignment.
accessibility_across_viewports: ul/li for screen readers. Each card focusable via Tab. Hidden scrollbar but scroll still works via keyboard (Arrow keys) and touch.
edge_cases_handled: Few items (less than visible count) — items just fit, no scroll
Single item — full width minus padding
Long card content — internal handling
RTL — flex-direction works correctly
what_breaks_naive_approach: Whole-card carousels don't show scroll affordance — users don't know more exists. Fixed counts don't adapt to viewport.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Partial card peek as scroll affordance. 2) Visible count scales with viewport. 3) Native scroll-snap, no JS.

title: Tablet hover-on-trackpad triggers desktop-style UI; tablet touch-only stays mobile
viewport_spectrum: iPad without keyboard (touch), iPad with Magic Keyboard trackpad (hybrid)
scenario_context: iPad with Magic Keyboard has trackpad — user gets hover capability. App should reactively switch UI between touch-optimized and hover-enabled modes based on whether trackpad is attached.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: UA-sniffing iPad and forcing touch UI
responsive_reasoning: 1. iPad input capability is dynamic — keyboard attaches/detaches.\n2. (hover: hover) and (any-pointer: fine) signal trackpad presence.\n3. UI elements like hover preview cards enable when capability exists.

detection_strategy.code_pattern: const canHover = useMediaQuery('(hover: hover) and (pointer: fine)');

layout_strategy.content_priority: Core features work everywhere; hover features enhance
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Hover capability adds preview on hover

interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Trackpad attached: hover preview. Detached: tap only.

performance_notes: Preview only renders when hovered/focused. Listeners only attached when capability allows. Reactive to trackpad attachment.
accessibility_across_viewports: Link always works (tap or click). Preview is enhancement. Focus-visible parity for keyboard.
edge_cases_handled: User attaches Magic Keyboard — capability changes, hover available next paint
User detaches keyboard — capability changes, preview hides
Hybrid touchscreen laptop — both available, preview shown on hover
Screen reader user — preview content available via aria-describedby (not shown but recommended)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hardcoding 'mobile = no hover' misses iPad+trackpad users. Hardcoding 'all desktops have hover' misses touchscreen laptops.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Reactive to attachment changes. 2) Capability-driven enhancement. 3) Core feature always works.

title: Notion-style inline blocks that work as columns on desktop and stack on mobile
viewport_spectrum: Notion document with column blocks
scenario_context: Notion has column blocks — desktop shows 2 or 3 columns, mobile stacks them vertically. Same content, layout adapts.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Fixed column counts regardless of viewport
responsive_reasoning: 1. Columns are a desktop affordance for horizontal space.\n2. Mobile vertical-only layout matches content order.\n3. Single block tree.
performance_notes: Pure CSS. min-w-0 prevents flex children from overflowing.
accessibility_across_viewports: DOM order respected; screen readers read columns in order.
edge_cases_handled: 2-column on narrow — stacks
3-column on wide — 3 across
Many columns — wrap with flex-wrap (production)
Image/text in column — internal sizing works
what_breaks_naive_approach: Fixed flex-row at all sizes makes columns cramped at narrow viewports.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Container-driven flex direction. 2) Single component for any column count. 3) Content order preserved.

title: Sticky pagination footer that respects mobile bottom nav and desktop layout
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (above bottom nav), tablet/desktop (below table)
scenario_context: Table pagination footer. On mobile, must clear bottom nav. On desktop, sits at table's bottom edge.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Pagination hidden behind bottom nav on mobile
Fixed bottom positioning that doesn't account for safe areas
responsive_reasoning: 1. Mobile: pagination sticky to table content bottom, with margin for bottom nav.\n2. Desktop: regular position.\n3. Safe area inset for home indicator.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap page buttons above bottom nav

performance_notes: Sticky positioning, no JS.
accessibility_across_viewports: Pagination always reachable.
edge_cases_handled: Mobile with bottom nav — pagination sits above
Desktop — pagination at table bottom
Tablet — adapts based on whether bottom nav present
what_breaks_naive_approach: Pagination hidden behind bottom nav on mobile = bug. Hard-coded heights break with safe area changes.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) CSS variable based positioning. 2) Safe area aware. 3) Adapts to app shell state.

title: Searchable select that uses native picker on mobile and combobox on desktop
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (native select), desktop (custom combobox)
scenario_context: A 'select country' field with 200+ options. Mobile users get native iOS/Android picker (better UX). Desktop users get custom combobox with type-to-filter (more efficient for many options).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Custom dropdown on mobile (worse than native)
Native select on desktop with 200 options (no search)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Native picker is OS-tuned on mobile.\n2. Custom combobox enables filtering on desktop.\n3. Same data, two UIs.

layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Combobox can filter; native picks one

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap to open native picker, scroll/tap to select
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Click to open, type to filter, click to select
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab to focus, type to filter, arrow keys, Enter to select

performance_notes: Native select on mobile = zero JS. Desktop combobox filters on each keystroke.
accessibility_across_viewports: Native select is fully accessible. Combobox uses listbox + option pattern with aria-selected.
edge_cases_handled: iPad with trackpad — combobox
iPad without — native select
Many options — combobox filters; native scrolls
Keyboard nav — both work
what_breaks_naive_approach: Custom dropdown on iOS is worse than native picker. Native select with 200 options on desktop has no filter.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) OS-native picker where it shines. 2) Filterable combobox where keyboard helps. 3) Pointer-aware swap.

title: Audio player with adaptive layout: compact mobile, full desktop, sticky during scroll
viewport_spectrum: All viewports — same audio playback, different controls layout
scenario_context: Music player. Mobile: compact bar at bottom (title + play + progress dots). Desktop: full controls (title + artist + album art + play + scrubber + volume). Sticky as user scrolls.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Mobile shows essential controls; desktop shows full chrome.\n2. State (current time, playing) shared across.\n3. Sticky bar that respects safe area.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Adaptive player UI with shared audio element
layout_strategy.content_priority: Play/pause + title always visible; secondary controls per viewport
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Desktop adds album art + artist + volume

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap play, swipe scrubber, tap mini bar to expand to fullscreen player
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover scrubber for preview, click to seek

performance_notes: Single <audio> element, reused. Controls vary; state shared via React.
accessibility_across_viewports: All controls labeled. Range inputs for scrubber/volume have aria-label. Play/pause button uses native control as base.
edge_cases_handled: Mobile bar tap → expand to fullscreen player (production feature)
Desktop scrub via mouse drag
User changes viewport mid-playback — UI swaps, audio continues
Safe area on iPhone — mini bar clears home indicator
what_breaks_naive_approach: Cramming desktop controls on mobile loses scrub precision. Hiding scrub loses seek.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Player chrome adapts to viewport. 2) Single audio state. 3) Safe area awareness.

title: Tabs that scroll horizontally when overflowing and use pills layout when fitting
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (scroll), tablet/desktop (fit)
scenario_context: Tab bar with 8 tabs. Desktop: all fit. Mobile: only 3 fit; rest accessible via horizontal scroll with scroll indicator gradient.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Wrapping tabs to multiple lines (loss of nav clarity)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Tabs are a horizontal navigation pattern.\n2. When they overflow, horizontal scroll preserves model.\n3. Gradient mask indicates more tabs exist.

detection_strategy.how_to_detect: CSS overflow-x: auto + native overflow handling

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Horizontal scroll with snap and mask gradient
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Mobile shows partial tabs; desktop all

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Swipe horizontally to see more tabs

performance_notes: Native horizontal scroll. scrollIntoView built-in. CSS scrollbar hidden.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=tablist/tab. aria-selected on active. 44pt min-h. Active tab auto-scrolls into view (helpful for keyboard nav).
edge_cases_handled: More tabs than fit — scroll with gradient indicators
All fit — gradients still visible but harmless
Keyboard arrow nav — Tab moves focus (default), arrow keys could be added for tablist pattern (production)
RTL — works with reversed scroll
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping tabs to 2 lines is visually confusing. Hidden overflow without indicators leaves users wondering if more tabs exist.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Horizontal scroll preserves tab model. 2) Active tab auto-scrolls into view. 3) Gradient masks indicate overflow.
